paragraphs.body: One craft subject that I think I learned about overall, and as broad as this may be, is plot. I am very interested in fictional works such as video games (especially those with deep lore), anime, TV, movies, etc. but have never been much of a writer or reader. I think a big thing I learned, especially while being new to creative writing, is not trying to make some revolutionary work of art. I really enjoyed writing my own stories and using the creative side of my brain, but I think I tried to be too inventive at first. I really learned that not every subtle detail you think you’re throwing in will be seen, because I’m not Shakespeare and no one will be analyzing my story that deeply.

annotations.comment: Ha! I appreciate this — I do think it’s very good to be ambitious, but yes, the basics are important (and actually quite hard on their own anyway).

paragraphs.body: Going along with this idea of trying to do too much, I think I was trying to compare myself to these famous/popular works of media I enjoy so much. I also think after reading some of my classmates stories, I realized that when it comes to reading instead of visualized media, I am not a fan of super intricate stories either. When workshopping, some people’s stories were just hard for me to enjoy because they were too long, wordy, or complicated when it came to setting, time, etc. During my second short story, I really tried to make something that was just more simple to follow and fun yet still surprising, unlike my first. In my first I think I just got too carried away trying to create a really cool and intricate story line while also trying to share a good life message/theme.

annotations.comment: That’s fair. You can tell why my advice is to simplify.

paragraphs.body: Anne Lamott in “Bird by Bird” states, “Plot grows out of character. If you focus on who the people in your story are… something is bound to happen.” She also states, “You are not going to give us the plans to the submarine. Life is not a submarine. There are no plans.” I feel like both of these quotes kind of focus on my point, that you cannot force an over complicated plot to create some revolutionary work of art. If you are able to make good characters, the plot will carry itself through their interactions and personalities. I think I was able to portray my characters pretty well through casual dialogue, but maybe if I was able to add more characters into my stories, they could have driven the plot better themselves.

annotations.comment: Yep. I think we often think we’ll write something new that no one else has done before, but in fact, most everything has been done already.

paragraphs.body: A class reading that helped me was “The Lottery” by Shirley Jackson. Although the character names and dialogue were a little difficult to follow on the first read, I think this story’s generally linear plot, short period of time, character descriptions through dialogue, and end twist really exemplify my idea of an enjoyable plot. I think if the story tried to span over several days, greatly focus on multiple years/lotteries, or included actual accounts/interactions with outside towns, the story would not have worked the same. It followed a straight forward plot of interactions and events leading to a shocking ending, that was hinted at throughout.

annotations.context_left: dialogue were a little difficult to follow on the first read, I think this story’s generally linear
annotations.context_right: , short period of time, character descriptions through dialogue, and end twist really exemplify my i

paragraphs.body: An additional reading from The Best American Short Stories 2020, “Kennedy” by Kevin Wilson, I feel is also a solid example of this concept. The story follows the complex relationship between two boys and a new kid they started being “friends” with named Kennedy. Kennedy’s character was quite dislikable, as I would consider him the antagonist of this story. I enjoyed this reading because it followed the pretty simplistic life of teenage boys (something I’m not far removed from) in high school but introduces some conflict through the personality of Kennedy and his interactions with the other two boys. I feel the timid personalities of the two friends interacting with Kennedy’s aggressiveness drive the story forward through some tense, awkward, and anxious moments, fitting for a group of teenage boys. Although there were a few other shocking/suspenseful moments, the news/twist at the end, of Kennedy shooting his own father, provided a tipping point for Kennedy’s unhinged behavior, which I thought was done well.

annotations.comment: That’s a hell of a story!
annotations.context_left: between two boys and a new kid they started being “friends” with named Kennedy. Kennedy’s character
annotations.context_right: quite dislikable, as I would consider him the antagonist of this story. I enjoyed this reading beca

paragraphs.body: I think all of these points really helped me complete my final short story. Although I didn’t write anything suspenseful or dark, I feel my story had a straight forward plot with a shocking (and fun) ending, that was hinted throughout. I think it follows a sort of script described by these other examples. Learning to not try and reinvent the wheel, creating a plot that is easy for others to understand was definitely needed for me to grow as a writer.

annotations.context_left: for me to grow as a

paragraphs.body: One thing I would like to work on outside of this, is being able to teach readers valuable lessons. With both my stories, I tried to make main characters that learn something valuable about life, something that I personally like to read and see in the media that I consume. Although these short stories wouldn’t have time to show true character development, I feel the last paragraph to the last few lines can show that well enough, but I feel I had difficulty incorporating these lessons more heavily.

annotations.comment: It’s hard to write moralistic stories that are also good, in my experience. That doesn’t mean that you shouldn’t but just that it’s hard.

paragraphs.body: Overall, I think I really enjoyed learning about fiction and creating it myself. I think dialogue is quite an important part of these as well, but that just plays a part of character development, which also drives plot much more than forcing some magnificent idea that worked in your head before you put pen to paper. All aspects of a creative work of writing must be interconnected well enough to tell your story. I’m glad I am able to appreciate the media I love even more, now that I further understand the work and intricacies of the creative writing process.

annotations.comment: That’s cool! Thanks, Natalie, it was fun having you in class! Wishing you well.
